content.full_text:
Re:
Fatn Por-rrrcar PnacrrcEs CorraMrssroN
428 J Street . Suite 620. Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660. Fax (916) 322-0886
July 23,2003
Gregory V. Moser
Foley &Lardner
402W. Broadway, Suite 2300
San Diego, CA 92101-3542
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-03-147
Dear Mr. Moser:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Barry S.
Nussbaum regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act').I The Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it provides advice. (tn ,"
Oglesby (197 5) 1 FPPC Ops. 7 1 ; Govt. Code section 83 I 14.) Further, this letter is
limited solely to the provisions of the Act and should not be taken as advice or an opinion
regarding any other area of the law potentially implicated by your request for advice. For
instance, as you noted in your letter, Govemment Code section 1090 may apply. The
Commission has no authority to advise on section 1090.
Please note, with respect to your second question, we are treating it as a request
for informal assistance. This is because we do not know the specific terms of the
proposed lease renewal. Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
irnmunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83ll4;regulation
183 29 (c)(3), copy enclo sed.)
QUESTTONS
1. Will Mr. Nussbaum have a conflict of interest if his employer enters into an
agreement with the Del Mar Thoroughbred Club to operate kiosks at the Del Mar race
track, if Mr. Nussbaum does not make, participate in making, or influence the decision?
I Govemment Code sections 81000 - gl}l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code ofRegulations.


File No. A-03-147
Page No. 2
2. If Mr. Nussbaum's employer enters into the license agreement with the Del
Mar Thoroughbred Club, will Mr. Nussbaum have aconflict of interest in future
decisions on the lease of the track to the Del Mar Thoroughbred club?
CONCLUSIONS
l. The existence of a conflict of interest is premise d,, inpart, on the making and
participating in making or influencing of a goveflrmental decision. The Del Mar
Thoroughbred Club is a private corporation. Because Mr. Nussbaum will not make,
participate in making, or influence a govenrment decision that will have a reasonab\r
foreseeable material financial effect on his economic interests, he will not have a conflict
of interest.
2. Irrespective of the license agreement, Mr. Nussbaum will have a conflict of
interest in any governmental decision that will have areasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on his employer. Mr. Nussbaum will not have an economic interest in the
Del Mar Thoroughbred Club, however, solely by virtue of the license agreement to
operate kiosks.
FACTS
Mr. Nussbaum is president of the 22"d Distict Agricultural Association and a
member of the California Racetrack Leasing Commission and Califomia Racetrack
Authority.
Mr. Nussbaum is also the chief operating officer and astockholder in Digital
orchid ("Digital'), a corporation which provides high speed data transmission
technology for cell phones.
The Del Mar racetrack is leased to the Del Mar Thoroughbred Club (,,DMTC,) by
the State Racetrack Leasing Commission (on behalf of the z2"{District Agricultural
Association) under a2\-year lease which commenced in December 1989. DMTC is a
Califomia corporation. Under the lease, every five years either party may give notice
between January 1 and January 3l that it wishes to renegotiate the terms. the next
renegotiation window begins January l,2OO4.
During the racing season, DMTC is to participate in off-track wagering within.
California, as authorized by the Califomia Horseracing Law. DMTC is iuthorized to
grant "concessions or contracts for the operation of one or more of the business activities
permitted" under the lease, "other than concessions for food and beverage.,, In addition,
"[d]uring the race meeting, thoroughbred horseracing on the premiser *ith pari-mufuel
wagering on the results thereof shall be conducted only under the direct supervision and
management of [DMTC]."
Digital has asked DMTC to enter into a license agreement allowing Digital to
operate two temporary kiosks to be installed during the 2003 racingseu.on forltre


File No. A-03-147
Page No. 3
purpose of distributing to potential subscribers literature and inform ationregarding how
Youbet.com fnc.'s existing Intemet-based system can be accessed via cell piones and
other wireless devices for wagering and receiving live horseracing information. Digital
provides Youbet.com Inc. customers a software interface which ailo*r cell phones and
other wireless devices to access Youbet's system. Youbet and Digital share fixed
subscription fees paid by customers. Subscribers gain access to wagering and racing
information for tracks across the United States. However, under their existing agreement
with Youbet, Digital receives no share of anyrevenues derived from gamingty
subscribers anywhere in the United States. Indeed, because Digital tacts the necessary
gaming licenses, it is legallyprohibited from receiving any share of gaming revenues. As
indicated above, Mr. Nussbaum is an officer and a stockholder of the propJsed licensee,
Digital. In a telephone message from your office left on July 18, 2OO5,you confirmed
that Mr. Nussbaum's ownership interest in Digital is less than 10 p".."rt. Mr. Nussbaum
has no position or ownership interest in youbet.com Inc.
DMTC would acce,pt as consideration for the kiosk license the potential for users
of the system to increase wagering on DMTC-operated races, increasing revenues to
DMTC. Digital would pay all costs of installing equipping and operating the kiosks.
Youbet's system is already available to anyone with an Internet connection to wager at
racetracks throughout the United States. Because Digital's service is new, it is unknown
how much wagering through Youbet's system might increase as a result of the additional
convenience of wireless device access.
Thrc22"d District Agricultural Association and State Racehack Leasing
Commission will next meet in August 2003. However, you are not aware of any decision
which either orgaruzation will be asked to consider between today and the end of the
2003 tace'meeting regarding the kiosk license agreement. Underthe terms of,the lease , it
is DMTC's decision, as tenant, whether to grant a license to Digital. No action or
consent by the Association or Commission is required us r"sp"Cts the license agreement.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from makin g, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to influenc" a gove-mental deJision in
which the official has a financial interest. The Commission has adopted an eight-step
standard analysis for deciding whether an official has a disqualifuing conflict if interest
(regulation 18700, subdivisions (bxl) - (8), which is discussed below. The general rule,
however, is that a conflict of interest may exist whenever apublic official *uf", u
govemmental decision which will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
on one or more of his or her financial interests.
1. Is the individual a public official?
The conflict-of-interest prohibition only applies to public officials. you
acknowledge that Mr. Nussbaum is a public official as a member of the 22"d Distict


File No. A-03-147
Page No. 4
Agricultural Association, the California Racetrack Leasing Commission, and the
california Racetrack Authority. (Section 82048 ; regulation 1 g70 I (a).)
2. will the official make, participate in making or influence a governmental
decision?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to disqualiff a public official
from "making" "patticipatingin making," or "influencing,, u gor"--intal decision.
Making a Governmental Decision: lJnder the analysis prescribed by step two, a
public official "makes a goveflrmental decision" when the official, actrngwithin the
authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, appoints u p"rror, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Section 87100; regulation l87\2.l(a).)
Participating in Making a Governmental Decision: Apublic official "participates
in making a govemmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his oi h"t
position and without significant substantive or intervening review, thL official negotiates,
advises or makes recommendations to the decision-maker regarding the governmental
decision. (Section 87 I 00; regulation 187 02.2.)
Influencing a Governmental Decision: There are two rules as to whether a public
official uses or attempts to use his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision. The first rule applies when the governmental decision is within orbefore the
public official's own agency, or an agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary
control of the public official's agency, but the public official is not a decision--maker per
se. (Regulation 18702.3(a).) In these cases, if "the official contacts, or appears befori or ,,
otherwise attempts to influence, any member, officer, employee or consuliant ofthe
agenct''then he or she is attempting to influence a govemmental decision. This includes,
but is not limited to, "appearances or contacts by the official on behalf of a business
entity, client, or customer."
The second rule applies when the govemmental decision is within or before an
agency other than the public official's own agency, or an agency appointed by or subject
to the budgetary conkol of the public official's agency. (Regulation 18702.3(b).) Unaer
. this rule, the official cannot act or purport "to acton behalf of or as the representative of,
his or her agency to any member, officer; employee or consultant of an agincy''to
influence a decision that will have a material financial effect on his orheieconomic
interests.
According to your facts, the kiosk decision will be made by DMTC, a private
corporation,not a govemmental agency. No governmental action will occur. In addition,
Mr' Nussbaum will not be influencing any other governmental entity regarding a decision
on the kiosk license. Since this decision on the kiosk license is not a governm-ental


File No. A-03-147
Page No. 5
decision, then the conflict-of-interest rules are not implicated and the analysis ends.2 In
short, under your facts, Mr. Nussbaum will not have any role in the decision regarding
the kiosks, and therefore he has no conflict of interest in the private corporation,s
decision.
However, you have also asked about other future decisions before government
agencies on which Mr. Nussbaum serves thatmay affectDMTc. Mr. Nussbaum wishes
to make and participate in these decisions. For these reasons, we will continue the
analysis under the Act. However, as noted above, the remainder of this letter is informal
assistance.
3. What are the officialrs economic interests?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising
from economic interests. These economic interests are described in regulations lg703.l
through 18703.5, inclusive. There appear to be three economic interests in question.
There are six possible economic interests:
1. A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or
she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more. (Section SIiOZ(a1;regulation
18703.1(a).)
2. A public official has an economic interest in which he or she is a director,
officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds anyposition ofmanagement. (Section
87 I 03(d); regulation I 8703. I (b).)
3. A public official.has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision. (Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.) The definition of "income,,includes a
pro rata share of any income of anybusiness entity in which the official owns a l0-
percent interest or greater. (Section 82030(a).)
4. A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family. lhis is
known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103, regulationig703.5.)
According to your facts, Mr. Nussbaum has an interest in Digital based on section
87103(a), (c) and (d). However, because he owns less than 10 percent of the company,
he does not have an economic interest in the clients of Digital.
4. WilI this economic interest be directly or indirectly involved in these decisions?
A person, including a business entity, in which a public official has an economic
interest is directly involved in a governmental decision if that person, either directly or by
2 Please note that the definitions in the Act are broadly conskued in order to fulfill its purposes to
avoid conflicts of interest in governmental decision making.


File No. A-03-147
Page No. 6
an agent, initiates the proceeding by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar
request, or is a named party in, or is the subject of the proceeding to be decided by the
official or the official's agency. (Regulation ls704.l(a)(t) - (a)e).) A person is the
subject of a proceeding before the agency if the "decision involves the issuancg renewal,
approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract
with" that person. (Regulation 18704.1(a)QD If a person who is an economic interest to
a public of{icial is not directly involved in a governmental decision, the person is deemed
to be indirectly involved. (Regulation 18704.1(b).)
5-6. What is the applicable materiality standard and is it reasonably foreseeable
that the financial effect of the governmental decision upon your economic interests
wiII meet this materiality standard?
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable impact of a
governmental decision on a public official's economic interests is material. (Regulation
18700(a).) Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on an economic interest will be material,depending on the nafure of the
economic interest and whether that interest is directly or indirectly involved in the
agency's decision.
Materiality
A person who is a business entity or a solrce of income is directly involved in a
decision before a public official's agency when that person is a named pariy,or is the
subject of the proceeding in which the governmental decision will be made. A person is
the subject of the proceeding if the decision involves issuance, renewal, upp.or:ul, denial
or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contraot rvjth, thu person.
(Regulation 1870ailil.)
When a public official's economic interest is a business entity and the business
entity is indirectly involved in a governmental decision, the materiality standards of
regulation 18705.1(c) apply. (Regulation 13704.1(b).) Regulation I 8705. 1(c) ranks
business entities into four categories of size and applies a separate financial threshold to
each category to define what a material financial effect is. Generally, the largerthe size
of the business entity, the greater the financial effect that is deemed *mateial.,,
Assuming from your description that Digital is not listed on the New york or 
.
American Stock Exchanges, or on NASDAQ, and that it had net income of less than
$500,000 or earnings before taxes of less than $750,000 for its last fiscal year, the
appropriate materiality standard would be found at regulation 1 g705.1(c)(a):
"(A) The goveflrmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of
$20,000 or more; or,


File No. A-03-147
Page No. 7
"(B) The goveflrmental decision will result in the business entity
incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating
existing expenses for a fiscal year inthe amount of $5,000 or more; or,
"(C) The govemmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more.,,
A decision in which Digital is indirectly involved will be considered as having a
material financial effect if any of the foregoing standards under regulation 18705.11cy+y
are met.3
Nexus
Notwithstanding the materiality standard set forth above, a separate standard may
also apply under certain circumstances:
"(c) Nexus. Any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on
a person who is a source of income to a public official is deemed
material if the public official receives or is promised the income to
achieve a goal orpurpose which would be achieved, defeated,
aided, or hindered by the decision.,,(Regulation 18705.3(c).)
The rationale for the nexus test is that when an employee eiuns a salary to
accomplish a pu{pose that may be advanced by what he or she does as a public official,
we presume that the ernployer is benefiting from the actions of the employee in his or her
official capacity. (Yarnell Advice Letter, No. 4-00-161.)
Tlpically a'hexus": is found in situations where the official is also a high-level
employee with direct influence and control over his or her employer's management or
policy decisions. (Low Advice Letter, No. 4-99-304.) Here, Mr. Nussbaum is a high-
level employee of Digital, whose job responsibilities explicitly involve him in Digiial,s.
policy decisions. Thus, a nexus exists, and arnaterial financial effect may occur, if a
particular govemmental decision he is asked to participate in would also achieve, defeat,
aid or hinder a policy, purpose, or goal of Digital.a (Nack Advice Letter, No. A-01-l2l;
Lucas Advice Letter, No. 4-96-248.)
3 This assumes that we have correctly identified Digital's size. If Digital is listed on one of the
above-identified exchanges, or has net income or earnings before taxes in an amount that places Digital
within either subdivisions (c)(2) or (c)(3) of regulation 18705. 1, the materiality standard under the
appropriate subdiyision would serve as a threshold, and not the standard described above.
a Please note that regulation 18705(c) provides an exception. It states: "special Rules.
NotwithstandingTitle 2,Califomia Code of Regulations, sections 18705.1 tkough 18705.5, inclusive, an
official does not have to disqualify himself or herself from a govemmental decision if: ( I ) Although a
conflict of interest would otherwise exist under Title2, Califomia Code of Regulations, sections 18705.1
through 18705.4, inclusive, and 18706, the decision will have no financial effect on the person or business
entity who appears before the official, or on the real property.,,


File No. A-03-147
Page No. 8
The nexus test is fact dependent. While it would not appear
Mr. Nussbaum would have anexus regarding the DMTC lease, facts may arise at
the time thatthe lease is considered thatmay create a nexus. For example, a
clause in the agreement prohibiting kiosks on the premises may defeat bigital,s
goal to market its product to race enthusiasts. However, without knowing the
precise terms of the agreement we cannot advise any further.
Reasonably Fores eeable
An effect upon economic interests is considered reasonably foreseeable if there is
a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) A financial effect need
not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere
possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.) Whether a material financial effect
is reasonably foreseeable is a factual question to be answered in light of the
circumstances sturounding each decision.
7. & 8. The 66Public Generally'o and o'Legally Required Participationrt Exceptions
An official who otherwise has a conflict of interest in a decision may still
participate under the "public generally'' exception. This exception applies when the
financial effect of a decision on a public official's economic interests is substantially the
same as the effect on a significant segment of the public. Step eight is an exceptiolthat
applies when the official is legallyrequired to participate in the decision. Tpically this
exception applies when the participation of a public official who has a conflict of interest
is required in order to convene a quorum of the public body. You provide no facts
indicating that either of these exceptions applies.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
tuisa Menchaca
Counsel
John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division
Jwwjg

sections.analysis:
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from makin g, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to influenc" a gove-mental deJision in
which the official has a financial interest. The Commission has adopted an eight-step
standard analysis for deciding whether an official has a disqualifuing conflict if interest
(regulation 18700, subdivisions (bxl) - (8), which is discussed below. The general rule,
however, is that a conflict of interest may exist whenever apublic official *uf", u
govemmental decision which will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
on one or more of his or her financial interests.
1. Is the individual a public official?
The conflict-of-interest prohibition only applies to public officials. you
acknowledge that Mr. Nussbaum is a public official as a member of the 22"d Distict
Agricultural Association, the California Racetrack Leasing Commission, and the
california Racetrack Authority. (Section 82048 ; regulation 1 g70 I (a).)
2. will the official make, participate in making or influence a governmental
decision?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to disqualiff a public official
from "making" "patticipatingin making," or "influencing,, u gor"--intal decision.
Making a Governmental Decision: lJnder the analysis prescribed by step two, a
public official "makes a goveflrmental decision" when the official, actrngwithin the
authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, appoints u p"rror, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Section 87100; regulation l87\2.l(a).)
Participating in Making a Governmental Decision: Apublic official "participates
in making a govemmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his oi h"t
position and without significant substantive or intervening review, thL official negotiates,
advises or makes recommendations to the decision-maker regarding the governmental
decision. (Section 87 I 00; regulation 187 02.2.)
Influencing a Governmental Decision: There are two rules as to whether a public
official uses or attempts to use his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision. The first rule applies when the governmental decision is within orbefore the
public official's own agency, or an agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary
control of the public official's agency, but the public official is not a decision--maker per
se. (Regulation 18702.3(a).) In these cases, if "the official contacts, or appears befori or ,,
otherwise attempts to influence, any member, officer, employee or consuliant ofthe
agenct''then he or she is attempting to influence a govemmental decision. This includes,
but is not limited to, "appearances or contacts by the official on behalf of a business
entity, client, or customer."
The second rule applies when the govemmental decision is within or before an
agency other than the public official's own agency, or an agency appointed by or subject
to the budgetary conkol of the public official's agency. (Regulation 18702.3(b).) Unaer
. this rule, the official cannot act or purport "to acton behalf of or as the representative of,
his or her agency to any member, officer; employee or consultant of an agincy''to
influence a decision that will have a material financial effect on his orheieconomic
interests.
According to your facts, the kiosk decision will be made by DMTC, a private
corporation,not a govemmental agency. No governmental action will occur. In addition,
Mr' Nussbaum will not be influencing any other governmental entity regarding a decision
on the kiosk license. Since this decision on the kiosk license is not a governm-ental
decision, then the conflict-of-interest rules are not implicated and the analysis ends.2 In
short, under your facts, Mr. Nussbaum will not have any role in the decision regarding
the kiosks, and therefore he has no conflict of interest in the private corporation,s
decision.
However, you have also asked about other future decisions before government
agencies on which Mr. Nussbaum serves thatmay affectDMTc. Mr. Nussbaum wishes
to make and participate in these decisions. For these reasons, we will continue the
analysis under the Act. However, as noted above, the remainder of this letter is informal
assistance.
3. What are the officialrs economic interests?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising
from economic interests. These economic interests are described in regulations lg703.l
through 18703.5, inclusive. There appear to be three economic interests in question.
There are six possible economic interests:
1. A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or
she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more. (Section SIiOZ(a1;regulation
18703.1(a).)
2. A public official has an economic interest in which he or she is a director,
officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds anyposition ofmanagement. (Section
87 I 03(d); regulation I 8703. I (b).)
3. A public official.has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision. (Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.) The definition of "income,,includes a
pro rata share of any income of anybusiness entity in which the official owns a l0-
percent interest or greater. (Section 82030(a).)
4. A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family. lhis is
known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103, regulationig703.5.)
According to your facts, Mr. Nussbaum has an interest in Digital based on section
87103(a), (c) and (d). However, because he owns less than 10 percent of the company,
he does not have an economic interest in the clients of Digital.
4. WilI this economic interest be directly or indirectly involved in these decisions?
A person, including a business entity, in which a public official has an economic
interest is directly involved in a governmental decision if that person, either directly or by
2 Please note that the definitions in the Act are broadly conskued in order to fulfill its purposes to
avoid conflicts of interest in governmental decision making.
an agent, initiates the proceeding by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar
request, or is a named party in, or is the subject of the proceeding to be decided by the
official or the official's agency. (Regulation ls704.l(a)(t) - (a)e).) A person is the
subject of a proceeding before the agency if the "decision involves the issuancg renewal,
approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract
with" that person. (Regulation 18704.1(a)QD If a person who is an economic interest to
a public of{icial is not directly involved in a governmental decision, the person is deemed
to be indirectly involved. (Regulation 18704.1(b).)
5-6. What is the applicable materiality standard and is it reasonably foreseeable
that the financial effect of the governmental decision upon your economic interests
wiII meet this materiality standard?
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable impact of a
governmental decision on a public official's economic interests is material. (Regulation
18700(a).) Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on an economic interest will be material,depending on the nafure of the
economic interest and whether that interest is directly or indirectly involved in the
agency's decision.
Materiality
A person who is a business entity or a solrce of income is directly involved in a
decision before a public official's agency when that person is a named pariy,or is the
subject of the proceeding in which the governmental decision will be made. A person is
the subject of the proceeding if the decision involves issuance, renewal, upp.or:ul, denial
or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contraot rvjth, thu person.
(Regulation 1870ailil.)
When a public official's economic interest is a business entity and the business
entity is indirectly involved in a governmental decision, the materiality standards of
regulation 18705.1(c) apply. (Regulation 13704.1(b).) Regulation I 8705. 1(c) ranks
business entities into four categories of size and applies a separate financial threshold to
each category to define what a material financial effect is. Generally, the largerthe size
of the business entity, the greater the financial effect that is deemed *mateial.,,
Assuming from your description that Digital is not listed on the New york or 
.
American Stock Exchanges, or on NASDAQ, and that it had net income of less than
$500,000 or earnings before taxes of less than $750,000 for its last fiscal year, the
appropriate materiality standard would be found at regulation 1 g705.1(c)(a):
"(A) The goveflrmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of
$20,000 or more; or,
"(B) The goveflrmental decision will result in the business entity
incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating
existing expenses for a fiscal year inthe amount of $5,000 or more; or,
"(C) The govemmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more.,,
A decision in which Digital is indirectly involved will be considered as having a
material financial effect if any of the foregoing standards under regulation 18705.11cy+y
are met.3
Nexus
Notwithstanding the materiality standard set forth above, a separate standard may
also apply under certain circumstances:
"(c) Nexus. Any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on
a person who is a source of income to a public official is deemed
material if the public official receives or is promised the income to
achieve a goal orpurpose which would be achieved, defeated,
aided, or hindered by the decision.,,(Regulation 18705.3(c).)
The rationale for the nexus test is that when an employee eiuns a salary to
accomplish a pu{pose that may be advanced by what he or she does as a public official,
we presume that the ernployer is benefiting from the actions of the employee in his or her
official capacity. (Yarnell Advice Letter, No. 4-00-161.)
Tlpically a'hexus": is found in situations where the official is also a high-level
employee with direct influence and control over his or her employer's management or
policy decisions. (Low Advice Letter, No. 4-99-304.) Here, Mr. Nussbaum is a high-
level employee of Digital, whose job responsibilities explicitly involve him in Digiial,s.
policy decisions. Thus, a nexus exists, and arnaterial financial effect may occur, if a
particular govemmental decision he is asked to participate in would also achieve, defeat,
aid or hinder a policy, purpose, or goal of Digital.a (Nack Advice Letter, No. A-01-l2l;
Lucas Advice Letter, No. 4-96-248.)
3 This assumes that we have correctly identified Digital's size. If Digital is listed on one of the
above-identified exchanges, or has net income or earnings before taxes in an amount that places Digital
within either subdivisions (c)(2) or (c)(3) of regulation 18705. 1, the materiality standard under the
appropriate subdiyision would serve as a threshold, and not the standard described above.
a Please note that regulation 18705(c) provides an exception. It states: "special Rules.
NotwithstandingTitle 2,Califomia Code of Regulations, sections 18705.1 tkough 18705.5, inclusive, an
official does not have to disqualify himself or herself from a govemmental decision if: ( I ) Although a
conflict of interest would otherwise exist under Title2, Califomia Code of Regulations, sections 18705.1
through 18705.4, inclusive, and 18706, the decision will have no financial effect on the person or business
entity who appears before the official, or on the real property.,,
The nexus test is fact dependent. While it would not appear
Mr. Nussbaum would have anexus regarding the DMTC lease, facts may arise at
the time thatthe lease is considered thatmay create a nexus. For example, a
clause in the agreement prohibiting kiosks on the premises may defeat bigital,s
goal to market its product to race enthusiasts. However, without knowing the
precise terms of the agreement we cannot advise any further.
Reasonably Fores eeable
An effect upon economic interests is considered reasonably foreseeable if there is
a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) A financial effect need
not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere
possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.) Whether a material financial effect
is reasonably foreseeable is a factual question to be answered in light of the
circumstances sturounding each decision.
7. & 8. The 66Public Generally'o and o'Legally Required Participationrt Exceptions
An official who otherwise has a conflict of interest in a decision may still
participate under the "public generally'' exception. This exception applies when the
financial effect of a decision on a public official's economic interests is substantially the
same as the effect on a significant segment of the public. Step eight is an exceptiolthat
applies when the official is legallyrequired to participate in the decision. Tpically this
exception applies when the participation of a public official who has a conflict of interest
is required in order to convene a quorum of the public body. You provide no facts
indicating that either of these exceptions applies.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Will Mr. Nussbaum have a conflict of interest if his employer enters into an
agreement with the Del Mar Thoroughbred Club to operate kiosks at the Del Mar race
track, if Mr. Nussbaum does not make, participate in making, or influence the decision?
I Govemment Code sections 81000 - gl}l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code ofRegulations.
2. If Mr. Nussbaum's employer enters into the license agreement with the Del
Mar Thoroughbred Club, will Mr. Nussbaum have aconflict of interest in future
decisions on the lease of the track to the Del Mar Thoroughbred club?

CONCLUSION: l. The existence of a conflict of interest is premise d,, inpart, on the making and
participating in making or influencing of a goveflrmental decision. The Del Mar
Thoroughbred Club is a private corporation. Because Mr. Nussbaum will not make,
participate in making, or influence a govenrment decision that will have a reasonab\r
foreseeable material financial effect on his economic interests, he will not have a conflict
of interest.
2. Irrespective of the license agreement, Mr. Nussbaum will have a conflict of
interest in any governmental decision that will have areasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on his employer. Mr. Nussbaum will not have an economic interest in the
Del Mar Thoroughbred Club, however, solely by virtue of the license agreement to
operate kiosks.

Further, this letter is limited solely to the provisions of the Act and should not be taken as advice or an opinion regarding any other area of the law potentially implicated by your request for advice. Please note, with respect to your second question, we are treating it as a request for informal assistance. This is because we do not know the specific terms of the proposed lease renewal. Will Mr. Nussbaum have a conflict of interest if his employer enters into an agreement with the Del Mar Thoroughbred Club to operate kiosks at the Del Mar race track, if Mr. Nussbaum does not make, participate in making, or influence the decision? I Govemment Code sections 81000 - gl}l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code ofRegulations. File No. A-03-147 Page No. 2 2. If Mr. Nussbaum's employer enters into the license agreement with the Del Mar Thoroughbred Club, will Mr. Nussbaum have aconflict of interest in future decisions on the lease of the track to the Del Mar Thoroughbred club? CONCLUSIONS l. The existence of a conflict of interest is premise d,, inpart, on the making and participating in making or influencing of a goveflrmental decision. The Del Mar Thoroughbred Club is a private corporation. Because Mr. Nussbaum will not make, participate in making, or influence a govenrment decision that will have a reasonab\r foreseeable material financial effect on his economic interests, he will not have a conflict of interest. 2. Irrespective of the license agreement, Mr. Nussbaum will have a conflict of interest in any governmental decision that will have areasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his employer. Mr. Nussbaum will not have an economic interest in the Del Mar Thoroughbred Club, however, solely by virtue of the license agreement to operate kiosks. FACTS Mr. Nussbaum is president of the 22"d Distict Agricultural Association and a member of the California Racetrack Leasing Commission and Califomia Racetrack Authority. Mr. Nussbaum is also the chief operating officer and astockholder in Digital orchid ("Digital'), a corporation which provides high speed data
